Question 1


Code
gauges <- tribble(
  ~site_no,     ~name,                            ~state,
  "09380000",   "Colorado R at Lees Ferry",       "AZ",
  "09163500",   "Colorado R near CO-UT border",   "CO",
  "09085000",   "Colorado R at Glenwood Springs", "CO",
  "09306500",   "White R near Watson",            "UT",
  "09379500",   "San Juan R near Bluff",          "UT",
  "09402500",   "Little Colorado R near Cameron", "AZ",
  "09423000",   "Colorado R below Hoover Dam",    "NV"
)

raw <- readNWISdv(
  siteNumbers = gauges$site_no,
  parameterCd = "00060",
  startDate   = "1980-01-01",
  endDate     = "2023-12-31"
) |>
  renameNWISColumns() |>
  left_join(gauges, by = "site_no") |>
  select(site_no, name, state, Date, Flow)

Question 2

site_no is the USGS station identifier shared between both tables
One-to-many. Each gauge has exactly one row in site_meta but thousands of daily flow records in raw
All flow records should be retained regardless of whether site metadata is available

The reason I chose left_join is so I could keep all of the flow records whether there was metadata or not. Site_no is the primary key in site_meta and the foreign key in raw which has lots of rows per gage.


Code
site_meta <- readNWISsite(gauges$site_no) |>
  select(
    site_no,
    drain_area_va,   # drainage area in square miles
    huc_cd,          # 8-digit HUC watershed code
    dec_lat_va,      # latitude
    dec_long_va      # longitude
  )

# Is site_no actually unique? If any row shows n > 1, the join will duplicate rows
site_meta |> count(site_no) |> filter(n > 1)

  mutate(
    raw_rank  = rank(-Flow),
    norm_rank = rank(-flow_per_sqmi),

c
The raw and normalized are quite different. The Lees Ferry ranks first by raw discharge due to the massive drainage but drops in the normalized ranking once you account for the actual size of the watershed. The little CO River near Cameron is the gage most likely to change since it has a small drainage area and has a high runoff intensity most likely due to convective storms. The normalization per area matter especially in CO because the drainage area are orders of magnitude different which can make the comparisons misleading. One analysis where I would use raw discharge is for compact accounting and normalized is best for comparing streamflow.
Dunne, T., & Leopold, L.B. (1978). Water in Environmental Planning. W.H. Freeman and Company, San Francisco.
Question 4
a and b


Code
# a. 7-day rolling mean per gauge
flow_meta <- flow_meta |>
  group_by(site_no) |>
  arrange(Date) |>
  mutate(roll7 = rollmean(Flow, k = 7, fill = NA, align = "right")) |>
  ungroup()

# b. 10th percentile threshold from 1980-2010 baseline, site-specific
thresholds <- flow_meta |>
  filter(Date >= "1980-01-01", Date <= "2010-12-31") |>
  group_by(site_no) |>
  summarize(threshold_p10 = quantile(Flow, 0.10, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

# c. Join thresholds and flag
flow_meta <- flow_meta |>
  left_join(thresholds, by = "site_no") |>
  mutate(below_threshold = roll7 < threshold_p10)




Code
# d - Lees Ferry plot
lees_ferry <- flow_meta |>
  filter(site_no == "09380000", Date >= "2000-01-01")

lees_threshold <- thresholds |>
  filter(site_no == "09380000") |>
  pull(threshold_p10)

ggplot(lees_ferry, aes(x = Date, y = roll7)) +
  geom_rect(
    data = lees_ferry |> filter(below_threshold),
    aes(xmin = Date, xmax = Date + 1, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf),
    fill = "red", alpha = 0.3, inherit.aes = FALSE
  ) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = lees_threshold, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  labs(
    title    = "Lees Ferry 7-Day Rolling Mean Discharge (2000–2023)",
    subtitle = "Red shading = below historical 10th percentile threshold",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "7-Day Rolling Mean (cfs)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

below_summary <- flow_meta |>
  filter(site_no == "09380000", Date >= "2000-01-01") |>
  mutate(period = case_when(
    year(Date) <= 2010 ~ "2000–2010",
    year(Date) >= 2011 ~ "2011–2023"

  filter(!is.na(below_threshold)) |>
  group_by(period) |>
  summarize(
    total_below   = sum(below_threshold, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_years       = n_distinct(year(Date)),
    days_per_year = total_below / n_years,
    .groups       = "drop"

below_summary |>

b


Code
wy_summary <- flow_meta |>
  group_by(site_no, name, water_year) |>
  summarize(
    total_flow    = sum(Flow, na.rm = TRUE),
    peak_flow     = max(Flow, na.rm = TRUE),
    days_below    = sum(below_threshold, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups       = "drop"
  )


c


Code
lees_wy <- wy_summary |>
  filter(site_no == "09380000")

lees_median <- median(lees_wy$total_flow, na.rm = TRUE)

ggplot(lees_wy, aes(x = water_year, y = total_flow,
                    fill = total_flow >= lees_median)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = lees_median, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray30") +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("TRUE" = "steelblue", "FALSE" = "firebrick"),
    labels = c("TRUE" = "Above median", "FALSE" = "Below median"),
    name   = NULL
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Lees Ferry Annual Total Discharge by Water Year (1980–2023)",
    subtitle = "Dashed line = long-term median",
    x        = "Water Year",
    y        = "Total Annual Discharge (cfs)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")









Annual discharge at Lees Ferry show a clear shift to below-median flows beginning WY 2000 with the deficit becoming increasingly persistent through the 2000s and 2010s. WY 2011 is the exception which has well above median flow. For the Compact accounting a sustained number of below median years means that less and less water is being delivered to Lake Powell and there is a significant water shortage.
Williams, A.P., et al. (2022). Rapid intensification of the emerging southwestern North American megadrought in 2020–2021. Nature Climate Change, 12, 232–234.
Question 6


Code
# Compute 1980-2010 baseline mean and SD per gage
baseline_stats <- wy_summary |>
  filter(water_year >= 1980, water_year <= 2010) |>
  group_by(site_no) |>
  summarize(
    baseline_mean = mean(total_flow, na.rm = TRUE),
    baseline_sd   = sd(total_flow, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups       = "drop"
  )

# Join back to full annual data and compute anomaly
wy_anomaly <- wy_summary |>
  left_join(baseline_stats, by = "site_no") |>
  mutate(anomaly = (total_flow - baseline_mean) / baseline_sd)

# Verify
nrow(wy_anomaly) == nrow(wy_summary)


[1] TRUE


Code
sum(is.na(wy_anomaly$anomaly))


[1] 0




Code
wy_anomaly |>
  mutate(name = str_wrap(name, width = 25)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = water_year, y = name, fill = anomaly)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_distiller(
    palette  = "RdBu",
    direction = 1,
    limits   = c(-3, 3),
    oob      = scales::squish,
    name     = "Standardized\nAnomaly (SD)"
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Basin-Wide Standardized Flow Anomaly (1980–2023)",
    subtitle = "Baseline = 1980–2010 mean ± SD per gauge",
    x        = "Water Year",
    y        = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.text.y  = element_text(size = 8),
    panel.grid   = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "right"
  )









The post-2000 drought signal appears consistently across nearly all gages. WY 2002 stands out as a basin-wide dry year. WY 2011 is a super wet year due to a deep Rocky Mountain snow pack. Simultaneous basin-wide anomalies reveal that drought stress is systemic rather than localized. A single gauge like Lees Ferry cannot confirm alone, since anomalies there could in principle reflect local tributary conditions rather than basin-wide deficits.

# Join coordinates to annual summary
wy_coords <- wy_summary |>
  left_join(
    site_meta |> select(site_no, dec_lat_va, dec_long_va),
    by = "site_no"
  )

# Verify
nrow(wy_coords) == nrow(wy_summary)

Code
# Compute flow-weighted mean lat/lon per water year
flow_center <- wy_coords |>
  group_by(water_year) |>
  summarize(
    w_lon = sum(dec_long_va * total_flow, na.rm = TRUE) / sum(total_flow, na.rm = TRUE),
    w_lat = sum(dec_lat_va  * total_flow, na.rm = TRUE) / sum(total_flow, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )




Code
# Western US basemap
western_states <- map_data("state") |>
  filter(region %in% c("arizona", "nevada", "utah", "colorado",
                        "new mexico", "california", "wyoming", "idaho"))

# Left panel: map with weighted center path
p_map <- ggplot() +
  geom_polygon(
    data = western_states,
    aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group),
    fill = "gray90", color = "white"
  ) +
  geom_path(
    data = flow_center,
    aes(x = w_lon, y = w_lat),
    color = "gray50", linewidth = 0.4
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = flow_center,
    aes(x = w_lon, y = w_lat, color = water_year),
    size = 2
  ) +
  scale_color_gradient(
    low  = "steelblue",
    high = "firebrick",
    name = "Water Year"
  ) +
  coord_fixed(1.3, xlim = c(-117, -104), ylim = c(31, 42)) +
  labs(
    title = "Flow-Weighted Center of Basin",
    x     = "Longitude",
    y     = "Latitude"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

# Right panel: weighted latitude over time with trend
p_lat <- ggplot(flow_center, aes(x = water_year, y = w_lat)) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue", linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "firebrick",
              linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(
    title = "Flow-Weighted Mean Latitude (1980–2023)",
    x     = "Water Year",
    y     = "Weighted Mean Latitude (°N)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

# Combine with patchwork
p_map + p_lat









The two northward spikes in weighted latitude most likely correspond to water years 1983 and 2011 both driven by Rocky Mountain snowpack delivered by strong El Niño conditions (1983) and an anomalously wet La Niña recovery year (2011), respectively. What they share is that both events produced outsized snowmelt pulses concentrated in the high-elevation northern headwaters of the Colorado and its upper tributaries, temporarily overwhelming the relative contribution of southern gauges like the Little Colorado and San Juan. The long-term upward trend in weighted latitude indicates that northern headwater gauges are becoming proportionally more important to total basin flow which is consistent with accelerating aridification of the southern tributaries due to persistent drought and warming. For water managers downstream, this matters because northern snowpack-dependent flows are highly variable and front-loaded in spring, making reliable year-round delivery increasingly difficult to guarantee under the Compact’s fixed annual accounting framework.
Question 8


Code
flow_meta <- flow_meta |>
  mutate(
    season = case_when(
      month %in% 4:6  ~ "Snowmelt",
      month %in% 7:9  ~ "Monsoon",
      month %in% 10:12 ~ "Low-flow",
      month %in% 1:3  ~ "Winter"
    ),
    season = factor(season, levels = c("Snowmelt", "Monsoon", "Low-flow", "Winter"))
  )




Code
seasonal_summary <- flow_meta |>
  group_by(site_no, name, water_year, season) |>
  summarize(
    seasonal_total  = sum(Flow, na.rm = TRUE),
    seasonal_norm   = seasonal_total / sum(drain_area_va, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups         = "drop"
  )




Code
# Identify peak 7-day mean date per gauge per water year
peak_timing <- flow_meta |>
  filter(!is.na(roll7)) |>
  group_by(site_no, water_year) |>
  slice_max(roll7, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(peak_doy = yday(Date))

# Plot Lees Ferry peak timing trend
peak_timing |>
  filter(site_no == "09380000") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = water_year, y = peak_doy)) +
  geom_point(color = "steelblue", size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE,
              color = "firebrick", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title    = "Lees Ferry Peak 7-Day Flow Timing (1980–2023)",
    subtitle = "Day of year of annual maximum 7-day rolling mean",
    x        = "Water Year",
    y        = "Peak Day of Year"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()









The trend line at Lees Ferry shows a shift toward earlier peak timing over the 44-year record, on the order of one to two weeks earlier by 2023 relative to 1980, consistent with warming-driven earlier snowmelt onset documented across the western US. The signal is real but partially obscured by year-to-year variability. Individual wet years like 2011 can push peak timing later and widen the scatter around the trend, so the statistical significance depends heavily on whether those outlier years are included. A two-week earlier peak timing is consequential for reservoir managers: Powell and Mead are designed to capture spring runoff during a narrow window, and an earlier pulse means storage operations must begin sooner or risk spilling water before reservoir capacity is available a particular problem in years when winter storage is already high from prior wet seasons, leaving less room to absorb an early snowmelt surge.
Question 9


Code
# Step 1: Lees Ferry annual flow
lees_annual <- wy_summary |>
  filter(site_no == "09380000") |>
  select(water_year, lees_flow = total_flow)

# Step 2: Glenwood Springs annual flow
glenwood_annual <- wy_summary |>
  filter(site_no == "09085000") |>
  select(water_year, glenwood_flow = total_flow)

# Step 3: Dominant season at Lees Ferry per water year
lees_dominant_season <- seasonal_summary |>
  filter(site_no == "09380000") |>
  group_by(water_year) |>
  slice_max(seasonal_total, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |>
  ungroup() |>
  select(water_year, dominant_season = season)

# Step 4: Join all three + log-transform
lees_model_data <- lees_annual |>
  left_join(glenwood_annual,       by = "water_year") |>
  left_join(lees_dominant_season,  by = "water_year") |>
  mutate(
    log_lees     = log(lees_flow),
    log_glenwood = log(glenwood_flow)
  )

# Verify: ~44 rows, zero NAs, log values positive
nrow(lees_model_data)

Code
lees_model_data |>
  ggplot(aes(x = log_glenwood, y = log_lees, color = dominant_season)) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2", name = "Dominant Season") +
  labs(
    title    = "Log-Log Model: Glenwood Springs → Lees Ferry",
    subtitle = "Slopes vary by dominant season (interaction model)",
    x        = "log(Glenwood Springs Annual Flow)",
    y        = "log(Lees Ferry Annual Flow)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()









The model should return an R² above 0.85, indicating that upstream Glenwood Springs flow and dominant season together explain the large majority of variance in annual Lees Ferry discharge, with the overall F-test highly significant (p << 0.001). The log_glenwood coefficient in a log-log model is an elasticity, or a coefficient greater than 1 means the upstream-downstream relationship is super-linear, so a 1% increase in Glenwood flow is associated with more than a 1% increase at Lees Ferry, which makes physical sense because tributary inflows between the two gauges compound an already-wet headwater year as water moves downstream. The significant negative interaction terms for non-Snowmelt seasons indicate that the upstream-downstream connection is weaker outside of the snowmelt season such as during Monsoon and Low-flow periods, tributary contributions are less synchronized with headwater conditions, local convective storms dominate individual gages, and basin depletions from irrigation diversions are proportionally larger, all of which decouple Lees Ferry from Glenwood Springs more than in a clean snowmelt year.
Question 10


Code
library(broom)

# a. Tidy model output
mod_aug <- augment(mod, data = lees_model_data)

# b. Predicted vs actual scatter
p_pred <- mod_aug |>
  ggplot(aes(x = .fitted, y = log_lees, color = dominant_season)) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0,
              linetype = "dashed", color = "gray30") +
  geom_text(
    data = mod_aug |> filter(abs(.resid) > 0.15),
    aes(label = water_year),
    nudge_y = 0.04, size = 3, color = "black"
  ) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2", name = "Dominant Season") +
  labs(
    title    = "Predicted vs. Actual: Log Lees Ferry Flow",
    subtitle = "Dashed line = perfect prediction; labeled points = large residuals",
    x        = "Fitted (log scale)",
    y        = "Actual (log scale)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

# c. Residual histogram
p_resid <- mod_aug |>
  ggplot(aes(x = .resid)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 15, fill = "steelblue",
                 color = "white", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed",
             color = "firebrick") +
  labs(
    title = "Model Residuals",
    x     = "Residual",
    y     = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p_pred + p_resid











Code
# One-row model summary
glance(mod) |>
  select(r.squared, adj.r.squared, p.value, AIC, nobs) |>
  flextable() |>
  colformat_double(digits = 3) |>
  set_header_labels(
    r.squared     = "R²",
    adj.r.squared = "Adj. R²",
    p.value       = "p-value",
    AIC           = "AIC",
    nobs          = "N"
  ) |>
  autofit()


R²Adj. R²p-valueAICN0.8550.8280.000-31.80845


The most likely outlier is water year 2011, which the model underpredicts because the exceptional snowpack that year produced flows well outside the range of the training data the model was fit on 30 years of increasingly drought-stressed conditions and simply has no precedent for that magnitude of coordinated basin-wide wetness. The residuals should appear approximately normal with a slight positive tail driven by that outlier and possibly one or two other anomalous wet years, which is a manageable violation but worth noting since it means prediction intervals will be slightly too narrow in extreme years. Two additional predictors that would most improve the model are April 1st SWE (snow water equivalent) at a representative Upper Basin SNOTEL station, which directly measures the snowpack reservoir that drives the dominant Snowmelt season and is the basis for USBR’s operational forecasts, and a Palmer Drought Severity Index or PDSI value for the Upper Basin the preceding autumn, which captures antecedent soil moisture conditions that control how much snowmelt reaches the channel versus recharging depleted soils before generating runoff.

title: Lab 2: Distances and Projections
Lab 2: Distances and Projections
text:
Question 3

3.1 Reference Map

big_cities <- cities |>
  slice_max(population, n = 10)

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = countries, fill = "grey85", color = "grey40", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_sf(data = states, fill = NA, color = "grey60", 
          linewidth = 0.3, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_sf(data = big_cities, size = 2, color = "navy") +
  geom_label_repel(
    data = big_cities,
    aes(geometry = geometry, 
        label = paste0(city, "\n", scales::comma(population))),
    stat = "sf_coordinates",
    size = 3,
    label.padding = unit(0.15, "lines"),
    max.overlaps = 20
  ) +
  coord_sf(xlim = st_bbox(states)[c(1,3)], ylim = st_bbox(states)[c(2,4)]) +
  theme_void() +
  labs(title = "10 Largest US Cities")










3.2 Distance to National Border

farthest_border <- cities |> slice_max(dist_to_border, n = 5)

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = states, fill = NA, color = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  geom_sf(data = cities, aes(color = dist_to_border), size = 0.3, alpha = 0.7) +
  gghighlight(
    dist_to_border >= min(farthest_border$dist_to_border),
    use_direct_label = FALSE,
    unhighlighted_params = list(alpha = 0.2, size = 0.2)
  ) +
  geom_label_repel(
    data = farthest_border,
    aes(geometry = geometry, label = city),
    stat = "sf_coordinates",
    size = 3,
    max.overlaps = 20
  ) +
  scale_color_viridis_c(name = "Distance (km)") +
  coord_sf(xlim = st_bbox(states)[c(1,3)], ylim = st_bbox(states)[c(2,4)]) +
  theme_void() +
  labs(title = "Distance to US National Border",
       subtitle = "5 farthest cities highlighted")

Warning: Using one column matrices in `filter()` was deprecated in dplyr 1.1.0.
ℹ Please use one dimensional logical vectors instead.
ℹ The deprecated feature was likely used in the gghighlight package.
  Please report the issue at
  <https://github.com/yutannihilation/gghighlight/issues>.


Warning: Could not calculate the predicate for layer 1; ignored









The cities farthest from the national border cluster are all in Kansas, all about 1000km from both the Atlantic/Gulf coasts and the Pacific coast. This makes sense given CONUS’s elongated east-west geometry. The geographic center of the lower 48 is in Kansas, so the interior plains maximize distance to any edge. A water resources application where border distance matters is transboundary surface water for farmers of the midwest or middle america.


3.3 Distance to Nearest State Border

farthest_state <- cities |> slice_max(dist_to_state, n = 5)

# Build a color palette so points and label boxes match
pal <- viridis::viridis(5)

# Assign colors based on rank order of dist_to_state
farthest_state <- farthest_state |>
  arrange(desc(dist_to_state)) |>
  mutate(pt_color = pal)

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = countries, fill = "grey20", color = "grey40", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_sf(data = states, fill = "grey30", color = "grey55", 
          linewidth = 0.2, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_sf(data = cities, aes(color = dist_to_state), size = 0.4, alpha = 0.9) +
  gghighlight(
    dist_to_state >= min(farthest_state$dist_to_state),
    use_direct_label = FALSE,
    unhighlighted_params = list(alpha = 0.15, size = 0.2, color = "grey60")
  ) +
  geom_label_repel(
    data = farthest_state,
    aes(geometry = geometry, label = city, 
        fill = pt_color, color = pt_color),
    stat = "sf_coordinates",
    size = 3,
    color = "white",           # text color
    label.padding = unit(0.2, "lines"),
    max.overlaps = 20,
    show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  scale_color_viridis_c(name = "Distance (km)") +
  scale_fill_identity() +      # use the hex colors directly
  coord_sf(xlim = st_bbox(states)[c(1,3)], ylim = st_bbox(states)[c(2,4)]) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "grey15", color = NA),
    legend.text = element_text(color = "white"),
    legend.title = element_text(color = "white"),
    plot.title = element_text(color = "white"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "white")
  ) +
  labs(title = "Distance to Nearest State Border",
       subtitle = "5 farthest cities highlighted")

Warning: Could not calculate the predicate for layer 1, layer 2; ignored









The state to border distance pattern is much more compressed than the national border pattern, the max distances have a sharp drop because the dense grid of state lines leaves little room to be far from all of them simultaneously. Cities in some western states like Nevada, Wyoming, Montana appear farthest from any state line. This indicates that western states are geographically expansive with fewer internal subdivisions than the dense East Coast. A hydrologist would use this metric to assess jurisdictional complexity of water allocation. A monitoring station or diversion point far from any state border falls cleanly within a single state’s water law framework. This makes permitting much simpler, whereas proximity to a border signals potential multi-state compact obligations.


3.4 Equidistance Zone: Mexico and Canada

cities <- cities |>
  mutate(dist_diff = abs(dist_to_mexico - dist_to_canada))

equidistant <- cities |>
  filter(dist_diff < 100) |>
  slice_max(population, n = 5)

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = countries, fill = "grey85", color = "grey40", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_sf(data = states, fill = NA, color = "grey60", 
          linewidth = 0.2, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_sf(data = cities, aes(color = dist_diff), size = 0.4) +
  gghighlight(
    dist_diff < 100,
    use_direct_label = FALSE,
    unhighlighted_params = list(alpha = 0.15, size = 0.2)
  ) +
  geom_label_repel(
    data = equidistant,
    aes(geometry = geometry, label = city),
    stat = "sf_coordinates",
    size = 3,
    max.overlaps = 20
  ) +
  scale_color_viridis_c(
    name = "|Mexico dist −\nCanada dist| (km)",
    direction = -1
  ) +
  coord_sf(xlim = st_bbox(states)[c(1,3)], ylim = st_bbox(states)[c(2,4)]) +
  theme_void() +
  labs(title = "Cities Approximately Equidistant from Mexico and Canada",
       subtitle = "Highlighted: within ±100 km of equal distance from both borders")

Warning: Could not calculate the predicate for layer 1, layer 2; ignored









The equidistant zone forms a sort of diagonal band that runs from the mid-Atlantic states through the Ohio Valley and into the central Plains. It is somewhat surprising that the band dips south through the interior rather than cutting across the northern tier; this occurs because Canada’s border curves southward around the Great Lakes, pulling the equidistant line further south in the Midwest than one would expect. Climatologically, cities in this corridor sit in a genuine transition zone where both Arctic air masses funneled south from Canada and Gulf moisture plumes that interact with systems in Mexico.

title: Lab 2: Distances and Projections
text:
Question 4

4.1 Quantify the Zone

total_pop <- sum(cities$population, na.rm = TRUE)

zone_summary <- cities |>
  st_drop_geometry() |>
  filter(dist_to_border < 160) |>
  summarise(
    n_cities     = n(),
    pop_in_zone  = sum(population, na.rm = TRUE),
    pct_of_total = (pop_in_zone / total_pop) * 100
  ) |>
  mutate(
    pop_in_zone  = scales::comma(pop_in_zone),
    pct_of_total = round(pct_of_total, 1)
  )

zone_summary |>
  flextable() |>
  set_header_labels(
    n_cities     = "Cities in Zone",
    pop_in_zone  = "Population in Zone",
    pct_of_total = "% of Total US City Population"
  ) |>
  autofit()

Cities in ZonePopulation in Zone% of Total US City Population13,345263,243,10965.7


The analysis finds that approximately 65.7% of the US city population lives within the 100-mile border zone. This aligns closely with the ACLU’s published estimate of roughly two-thirds (~67%) of all Americans. The result may surprise people because the zone is defined by all borders including along the great lakes and along the oceans. One scenario where a water resources professional might need to navigate federal jurisdictional boundary is maybe like the Columbia River which starts in Canada and goes through a lot of Canadian farmland (and potentially pesticides and other runoff), before making its way into the US where it is a vital part of the American Economy. The Clean Water Act is potentially another permitting boundary.

top_city_per_state <- cities |>

ggplot() +

[1] 36


There are 36 states that have at least one city in the zone. Ther are states where the most populous city is not in the zone such as Atlanta Georgia. For environmental monitoring, this jurisdictional boundary creates real issues. For example, a river basin or aquifer that straddles the 100-mile line may fall under different federal oversight regimes on either side, complicating integrated monitoring network design and data sharing agreements between state and federal agencies.

title: Lab 2: Distances and Projections
text:
Question 5

library(leaflet)

Warning: package 'leaflet' was built under R version 4.5.2

# Transform all layers back to WGS84 (EPSG:4326) — leaflet requires geographic
# coordinates, not projected. eqdc uses meters in a conic projection that
# leaflet cannot render directly.
cities_wgs <- cities |> st_transform(4326)
states_wgs  <- states |> st_transform(4326)

# Pull coordinates out for leaflet (faster than letting it extract from sf)
cities_wgs <- cities_wgs |>
  mutate(
    lon = st_coordinates(geometry)[, 1],
    lat = st_coordinates(geometry)[, 2]
  ) |>
  st_drop_geometry()

# Define the three city subsets
border_zone  <- cities_wgs |> filter(dist_to_border < 160)
equidist     <- cities_wgs |> filter(dist_diff < 100)
all_cities   <- cities_wgs

# Color palette: distance to national border
pal <- colorNumeric("viridis", domain = all_cities$dist_to_border, reverse = FALSE)

# Popup builder — shared across layers
make_popup <- function(df) {
  paste0(
    "<b>", df$city, ", ", df$state_name, "</b><br>",
    "Population: ", scales::comma(df$population), "<br>",
    "Dist. to national border: ", round(df$dist_to_border, 1), " km<br>",
    "Dist. to Mexico: ",          round(df$dist_to_mexico, 1), " km<br>",
    "Dist. to Canada: ",          round(df$dist_to_canada, 1), " km"
  )
}

leaflet() |>

  # --- Basemaps ---
  addProviderTiles(providers$CartoDB.DarkMatter,  group = "Dark") |>
  addProviderTiles(providers$CartoDB.Positron,    group = "Light") |>
  addProviderTiles(providers$Esri.WorldTopoMap,   group = "Topo") |>

  # --- State boundaries ---
  addPolygons(
    data        = states_wgs,
    fill        = FALSE,
    color       = "white",
    weight      = 0.6,
    opacity     = 0.4,
    group       = "State Boundaries"
  ) |>

  # --- Layer A: All cities colored by distance to border ---
  addCircleMarkers(
    data   = all_cities,
    lng    = ~lon, lat = ~lat,
    radius = 2,
    color  = ~pal(dist_to_border),
    stroke = FALSE,
    fillOpacity = 0.7,
    popup  = make_popup(all_cities),
    group  = "All Cities (border distance)"
  ) |>

  # --- Layer B: 100-mile border zone ---
  addCircleMarkers(
    data   = border_zone,
    lng    = ~lon, lat = ~lat,
    radius = 3,
    color  = "orange",
    stroke = FALSE,
    fillOpacity = 0.8,
    popup  = make_popup(border_zone),
    group  = "100-Mile Border Zone"
  ) |>

  # --- Layer C: Equidistance band (Mexico vs Canada ±100 km) ---
  addCircleMarkers(
    data   = equidist,
    lng    = ~lon, lat = ~lat,
    radius = 3,
    color  = "#00CFFF",
    stroke = FALSE,
    fillOpacity = 0.8,
    popup  = make_popup(equidist),
    group  = "Equidistance Zone (MX/CA)"
  ) |>

  # --- Legend ---
  addLegend(
    position = "bottomright",
    pal      = pal,
    values   = all_cities$dist_to_border,
    title    = "Distance to<br>National Border (km)",
    opacity  = 0.9
  ) |>

  # --- Layer controls ---
  addLayersControl(
    baseGroups    = c("Dark", "Light", "Topo"),
    overlayGroups = c(
      "State Boundaries",
      "All Cities (border distance)",
      "100-Mile Border Zone",
      "Equidistance Zone (MX/CA)"
    ),
    options = layersControlOptions(collapsed = FALSE)
  ) |>

  # Start with just the border zone visible to avoid overwhelming the map
  hideGroup("All Cities (border distance)") |>
  hideGroup("Equidistance Zone (MX/CA)")





The equidistant conic projection (eqdc) stores coordinates in meters relative to a custom projection center, which leaflet cannot interpret. The leaflet’s rendering engine expects longitude/latitude in WGS84 (EPSG:4326), so all sf layers must pass through st_transform(4326) before plotting. To keep the map nimble with over 30,000 points, coordinates are extracted once into plain numeric columns with st_coordinates() and geometry is dropped with st_drop_geometry() before passing to leaflet, avoiding repeated coordinate extraction during the rendering. This handled all the coodinates much faster. Some design choices I made were to use the three base map option so you could toggle between them, and to initially hide the densest layer maps.

# Get CONUS counties and project to Albers Equal Area (EPSG:5070)

counties <- AOI::aoi_get(state = "conus", county = "all") |>
  st_transform(5070) |>
  mutate(id = 1:n())

counties <- counties |> mutate(county_id = 1:n())





# Generate centroids, then union into a single MULTIPOINT
county_centroids <- st_centroid(counties)

Warning: st_centroid assumes attributes are constant over geometries

centroids_union <- st_union(county_centroids)   # → sfc MULTIPOINT





# ── Voronoi ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
voronoi <- st_voronoi(centroids_union) |>
  st_cast() |>                    # simplify GEOMETRYCOLLECTION → POLYGON
  st_as_sf() |>
  mutate(id = 1:n())

# ── Delaunay triangulation ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
triangulate <- st_triangulate(centroids_union) |>
  st_cast() |>
  st_as_sf() |>
  mutate(id = 1:n())

# ── Square grid (n = 70 cells along longest dimension) ─────────────────────
sq_grid <- st_make_grid(counties, n = 70, square = TRUE) |>
  st_as_sf() |>
  mutate(id = 1:n())

# ── Hexagonal grid ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
hex_grid <- st_make_grid(counties, n = 70, square = FALSE) |>
  st_as_sf() |>
  mutate(id = 1:n())





conus_boundary <- st_union(counties)   # single POLYGON / MULTIPOLYGON





# Report original vertex count
original_pts <- mapview::npts(conus_boundary)

# Simplify — retain 1% of vertices via Visvalingam (ms_simplify default)
conus_simple <- rmapshaper::ms_simplify(conus_boundary, keep = 0.01, keep_shapes = TRUE)

simplified_pts <- mapview::npts(conus_simple)

cat("Original vertices:  ", original_pts, "\n")

Original vertices:   11292 

cat("Simplified vertices:", simplified_pts, "\n")

Simplified vertices: 114 

cat("Points removed:     ", original_pts - simplified_pts, "\n")

Points removed:      11178 



# Check boundary is still valid
st_is_valid(conus_simple)

[1] TRUE

# Confirm dams (loaded later) still fall within simplified boundary
# dams_in_boundary <- st_filter(dams, conus_simple)
# nrow(dams_in_boundary) == nrow(dams)   # should be TRUE


# Voronoi & Delaunay → st_intersection() (tiles extend beyond CONUS)
voronoi_clip    <- st_intersection(voronoi,    conus_simple) |> st_cast()

Warning: attribute variables are assumed to be spatially constant throughout
all geometries

triangulate_clip <- st_intersection(triangulate, conus_simple) |> st_cast()

Warning: attribute variables are assumed to be spatially constant throughout
all geometries

# Square & Hexagonal grids → st_filter() (keep complete tiles touching CONUS)
sq_grid_clip  <- st_filter(sq_grid,  conus_simple)
hex_grid_clip <- st_filter(hex_grid, conus_simple)

After simplification using visvaligngam with the keep=0.01 the original CONUS boundary contained 11292 vertices and the simplified boundary had 114 vertices which is removing almost 99% or the original geometry. This reduction has serious computational consequence. It accelerates the st_intersection() calls used to clip the Voronoi and Delaunay tessellations. For the regular grids clipped with st_filter() the simiplfication doesnt have any effect though since this filtering only tests whether the tiles touch the boundary as opposed to computing the exact geometry. The tradeoff is some minor inaccuracy around the Great Lakes and some parts of the shoreline. This matters less since we are interested in Dams here and those aren’t on the ocean.




plot_tess <- function(sf_obj, title_str) {
  ggplot(data = sf_obj) +
    geom_sf(fill = "white", color = "navy", linewidth = 0.2) +
    theme_void() +

  st_as_sf() |>
  mutate(id = 1:n())

After simplification using visvaligngam with the keep=0.01 the original CONUS boundary contained 11292 vertices and the simplified boundary had 114 vertices which is removing almost 99% or the original geometry. For the regular grids clipped with st_filter() the simiplfication doesnt have any effect though since this filtering only tests whether the tiles touch the boundary as opposed to computing the exact geometry. The tradeoff is some minor inaccuracy around the Great Lakes and some parts of the shoreline. This matters less since we are interested in Dams here and those aren’t on the ocean.

The five tessellations differ a lot in the feature count, mean tile area and the variance. these differences end up shaping the results of any point in polygon analysis through MAUP. Counties exhibit the largest std dev of tile area in the dataset. The Voronoi tessellation anchors tile boundaries to county centroids which produces small tiles on the east coast and larger in teh west. The introduces a bias because it aggregates dams across multiple hydrologic regions which obscures variation. The Delaunay triangulation produces the most tiles and the smallest mean area which make the largest computational cost. The square and hexagonal grids have almost zero within tessallation variance by construction which eliminates the size driven MAUP artifact. This makes them the best choice for comparing dam density across regions. the hexagonal grid has better geometric advantage over square because there are 6 equidistant neighbors rather four for the square.

point_in_polygon <- function(points, polygons, id_col) {
  
  polygons_slim <- polygons |> select(all_of(id_col))
  
  polygons_slim |>
    st_join(points) |>
    st_drop_geometry() |>
    count(.data[[id_col]]) |>

    st_as_sf()

3.6 b/c
Two regions where tessellation choice produced substatially different pattern are the upper Midwest/Ohio River Valley and Colorado. The upper midwest has the densest dam concentration in CONUS. The county tessellation breaks the cluster into a bunch of small high count polygons that are aligned with county lines. This make really visually apparent but are artificially bounded by political geometry. The square and hexagonal grids aggregate the same concentration into a smaller number of large bright cells which conveys the regional magnitude while obscuring the county level variation. In Colorado River basin the county tessellation assigns super high dam counts to a small number of geographically huge counties which suggests an extreme concentration when the infrastructure is actually dispersed across a large river system across multiple states. the Delaunay triangulation with the smallest mean area and highest feature count subdivides this same space into many triangulations which does a better job at reflecting the distribution of the infrastructure. This is MAUP scale effect operating. For a dam manger focused on geographic clustering I think hexagonal grid would be the primary tessellation to recommend.

4.3
The hydroelectric dams are clustered in the Pacific Northwest and New England. Both regions tend to have high gradient and relatively high consistent discharge that make these economically viable. The absence of hydroelectric in the Great Plains or Southwest reflects either low topography or streamflow that’s too variable making it economically unviable. Irrigation dams have the a significant concentration in the west, particularly in Colorado, Idaho and California. These are places that can really have varying degrees or water ina given year and therefore rely on water impoundment particularly for agriculture. In the East there is either more consistent water or at least better groundwater storage to draw from. Hexagonal tessellation shapes all these patterns by averaging dam locations across equal-area tiles. This tends to suppress the western infrastructure clusters. If you made the grid finer is might make more cluster around the east coast but it would make the western tiles have more biased introduced and make them appear more empty.

library(tidyr)
# Spatial join dams to hex grid, summarize attributes per tile
tile_attrs <- hex_grid_clip |>
  st_join(dams) |>
  st_drop_geometry() |>
  group_by(id) |>
  summarise(

    mean_surface_acres = mean(surface_area_acres, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Join summaries back to geometry, preserve zero-dam tiles
hex_attrs <- hex_grid_clip |>
  left_join(tile_attrs, by = "id") |>
  mutate(
    n_dams             = replace_na(n_dams, 0),
    mean_year          = mean_year,          # NA is correct for empty tiles
    mean_surface_acres = mean_surface_acres
  )



5.2

library(spdep)

Warning: package 'spdep' was built under R version 4.5.3


Loading required package: spData


Warning: package 'spData' was built under R version 4.5.3


To access larger datasets in this package, install the spDataLarge
package with: `install.packages('spDataLarge',
repos='https://nowosad.github.io/drat/', type='source')`

# Build queen contiguity neighbor list
nb <- poly2nb(hex_attrs, queen = TRUE)

# Row-standardized weights matrix (W)
lw     <- nb2listw(nb, style = "W", zero.policy = TRUE)
W_std  <- listw2mat(lw)


# ── Attribute vectors ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
count_vec <- hex_attrs$n_dams
age_vec   <- hex_attrs$mean_year
size_vec  <- hex_attrs$mean_surface_acres

# ── NA imputation with global mean before matrix multiplication ────────────
impute_mean <- function(x) { x[is.na(x)] <- mean(x, na.rm = TRUE); x }

age_imp  <- impute_mean(age_vec)
size_imp <- impute_mean(size_vec)

# ── Spatial lag (W %*% attribute) ─────────────────────────────────────────
lag_count <- as.vector(W_std %*% count_vec)
lag_age   <- as.vector(W_std %*% age_imp)
lag_size  <- as.vector(W_std %*% size_imp)

# ── Correlate with spatial lag (only where real observations exist) ────────
cor_count <- cor(count_vec,               lag_count,
                 use = "complete.obs")
cor_age   <- cor(age_vec[!is.na(age_vec)], lag_age[!is.na(age_vec)],
                 use = "complete.obs")
cor_size  <- cor(size_vec[!is.na(size_vec)], lag_size[!is.na(size_vec)],
                 use = "complete.obs")

# ── Summary table ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
lag_summary <- data.frame(
  Attribute    = c("Dam count", "Mean year completed", "Mean surface area (acres)"),
  Correlation  = round(c(cor_count, cor_age, cor_size), 3),
  Strength     = case_when(
    abs(c(cor_count, cor_age, cor_size)) >= 0.5 ~ "Strong",
    abs(c(cor_count, cor_age, cor_size)) >= 0.2 ~ "Moderate",
    TRUE                                         ~ "Weak"
  )
)

knitr::kable(
  lag_summary,
  col.names = c("Attribute", "Spatial Lag Correlation", "Strength"),
  caption   = "Spatial lag correlations for dam attributes — Hexagonal Grid (n = 70)"
)

# Standardize dam count and its spatial lag
z_count     <- scale(count_vec,  center = TRUE, scale = TRUE)[, 1]
z_lag_count <- scale(lag_count,  center = TRUE, scale = TRUE)[, 1]

# Assign Moran quadrants
moran_df <- data.frame(
  z     = z_count,
  z_lag = z_lag_count
) |>
  mutate(quadrant = case_when(
    z >= 0 & z_lag >= 0 ~ "HH",
    z <  0 & z_lag <  0 ~ "LL",
    z >= 0 & z_lag <  0 ~ "HL",
    z <  0 & z_lag >= 0 ~ "LH"
  ))

# Quadrant counts
quad_counts <- count(moran_df, quadrant)
print(quad_counts)

  quadrant    n
1       HH  523
2       HL  111
3       LH  182
4       LL 1475

# Plot
ggplot(moran_df, aes(x = z, y = z_lag, color = quadrant)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, size = 1.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40") +
  geom_smooth(aes(group = 1), method = "lm", se = FALSE,
              color = "black", linewidth = 0.7) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c(HH = "#2166ac", LL = "#d1e5f0", HL = "#f4a582", LH = "#92c5de"),
    name   = "Quadrant"
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Moran Scatterplot — Dam Count",
    x        = "Standardized dam count (z)",
    y        = "Spatial lag of dam count (z)",
    caption  = paste0(
      "HH: ", quad_counts$n[quad_counts$quadrant == "HH"], "  ",
      "LL: ", quad_counts$n[quad_counts$quadrant == "LL"], "  ",
      "HL: ", quad_counts$n[quad_counts$quadrant == "HL"], "  ",
      "LH: ", quad_counts$n[quad_counts$quadrant == "LH"]
    )
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'











5.4

a. Spatial pattern
The spatial lag correlations from 5.2 show a clear hierarchy across the three dam attributes. Dam count shows the strongest spatial autocorrelation, likely exceeding r=0.5 which qualifies as a strong cluster. The neighboring hex tiles tend to have similarly high or low dam counts across large regions of CONUS. This makes geographic sense since rivers are continuous so a tile with lots of dams will typically border other tiles on the same river system. The mean year completed (r=0.2-0.5) indicates that there was a real time period where dams were being built. Mean surface area shows the weakest correlation of the three, mainly because dam placement is typically constrained by geology which is site specific.


b. Moran Scatterplot
The Moran scatter plot for dam count should show a pronounced concentration of points in the upper-right (HH) and lower-left (LL) quadrants with the OLS regression line which has a clear positive slope. the slope itself is the global Morans I statistic. this diagonal clustering indicates high cluster surrounded by other sparse tiles rather than the points being scattered randomly across all four quadrants. The HH cluster corresponds to the eastern dam belt while the LL cluster corresponds to the more arid interior where dam infrastructure is sparse across large areas. If age and surface area scatterplots were overlaid both would show weaker diagonal clustering and more scatter around the regression line.


c. Spatial Implications
The correlation values and Moran scatterplot confirm that neighboring hex tiles are genuinely similar in dam count and relatively similar in dam age. Which means the independence assumption underlying the OLS regression is violated. The drivers of the spatial infrastructure are fundamentally hydrologic and policy based rather than coincidentally. They also respond to agricultural and municipal demand which are also clustered by climate zone. these correspond to question 4 maps where irrigation dams cluster in the arid west and hydroelectric dams in the PNW, each purpose pattern is spatially structured since the age and size correlate with purpose.

# b. Remove self-neighbors
diag(W_hex) <- 0

# c. Row-standardize — divide each row by its row sum
row_sums       <- rowSums(W_hex)
row_sums[row_sums == 0] <- 1          # avoid divide-by-zero for isolated tiles
W_hex_std      <- W_hex / row_sums

# d. Compute spatial lag of dam count
n_hex     <- hex_dams$n
lag_hex   <- as.vector(W_hex_std %*% n_hex)

# e. Standardize both to z-scores
z_hex     <- scale(n_hex,   center = TRUE, scale = TRUE)[, 1]
z_lag_hex <- scale(lag_hex, center = TRUE, scale = TRUE)[, 1]

# Optional validation
cat("Tiles with no neighbors:", sum(rowSums(W_hex) == 0), "\n")

Tiles with no neighbors: 0 

cat("Row sums range (post-standardize):",
    round(range(rowSums(W_hex_std)), 3), "\n")

Row sums range (post-standardize): 1 1 




6.3

# Assign quadrants
hex_dams <- hex_dams |>
  mutate(
    z     = z_hex,
    z_lag = z_lag_hex,
    lisa  = case_when(
      z >= 0 & z_lag >= 0 ~ "HH",
      z <  0 & z_lag <  0 ~ "LL",
      z >= 0 & z_lag <  0 ~ "HL",
      z <  0 & z_lag >= 0 ~ "LH"
    )
  )

# Quadrant counts
hex_quad <- count(st_drop_geometry(hex_dams), lisa)
print(hex_quad)

  lisa    n
1   HH  523
2   HL  113
3   LH  169
4   LL 1486

# LISA color palette — consistent across both panels
lisa_colors <- c(
  HH = "#d7191c",   # red    — hotspot
  LL = "#2c7bb6",   # blue   — coldspot
  HL = "#fdae61",   # orange — high surrounded by low
  LH = "#abd9e9"    # cyan   — low surrounded by high
)

# a) Moran scatterplot
p_moran_hex <- ggplot(hex_dams, aes(x = z, y = z_lag, color = lisa)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, size = 1.2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40") +
  geom_smooth(aes(group = 1), method = "lm", se = FALSE,
              color = "black", linewidth = 0.7) +
  scale_color_manual(values = lisa_colors, name = "Quadrant") +
  labs(title = "Moran Scatterplot — Hex Grid",
       x = "Standardized dam count (z)",
       y = "Spatial lag (z)") +
  theme_minimal()

# b) LISA geographic map
p_lisa_hex <- ggplot(hex_dams) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = lisa), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = lisa_colors, name = "LISA quadrant") +
  theme_void() +
  labs(
    title   = "LISA Map — Hexagonal Grid",
    caption = paste0(
      "HH: ", hex_quad$n[hex_quad$lisa == "HH"], "  ",
      "LL: ", hex_quad$n[hex_quad$lisa == "LL"], "  ",
      "HL: ", hex_quad$n[hex_quad$lisa == "HL"], "  ",
      "LH: ", hex_quad$n[hex_quad$lisa == "LH"]
    )
  )



6.4

# ── County neighbor matrix ────────────────────────────────────────────────
W_county <- st_touches(county_dams, sparse = FALSE)
diag(W_county) <- 0

row_sums_c              <- rowSums(W_county)
row_sums_c[row_sums_c == 0] <- 1
W_county_std            <- W_county / row_sums_c

n_county     <- county_dams$n
lag_county   <- as.vector(W_county_std %*% n_county)

z_county     <- scale(n_county,   center = TRUE, scale = TRUE)[, 1]
z_lag_county <- scale(lag_county, center = TRUE, scale = TRUE)[, 1]

# Assign quadrants
county_dams <- county_dams |>
  mutate(
    z     = z_county,
    z_lag = z_lag_county,
    lisa  = case_when(
      z >= 0 & z_lag >= 0 ~ "HH",
      z <  0 & z_lag <  0 ~ "LL",
      z >= 0 & z_lag <  0 ~ "HL",
      z <  0 & z_lag >= 0 ~ "LH"
    )
  )

county_quad <- count(st_drop_geometry(county_dams), lisa)
print(county_quad)

  lisa    n
1   HH  756
2   HL  232
3   LH  317
4   LL 1803

# LISA map — counties
p_lisa_county <- ggplot(county_dams) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = lisa), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = lisa_colors, name = "LISA quadrant") +
  theme_void() +
  labs(
    title   = "LISA Map — Counties",
    caption = paste0(
      "HH: ", county_quad$n[county_quad$lisa == "HH"], "  ",
      "LL: ", county_quad$n[county_quad$lisa == "LL"], "  ",
      "HL: ", county_quad$n[county_quad$lisa == "HL"], "  ",
      "LH: ", county_quad$n[county_quad$lisa == "LH"]
    )
  )

# ── Side-by-side comparison ───────────────────────────────────────────────
(p_lisa_hex / p_lisa_county) +
  plot_annotation(
    title   = "LISA Hotspot Comparison: Hexagonal Grid vs. Counties",
    caption = "National Dam Inventory — USACE | EPSG:5070"
  )










6.5

a.
The LISA maps reveal HH hotspots concentrated most strongly in the Ohio Valley, Tennessee and New England. These are regions where dam density in any given tile is reinforced by equally dense neighboring tiles. Tennessee stands out as the most spatial contiguous HH clusters in CONUS. A couple reasons is that the river systems in Tennessee has high gradient and high discharge making is prime for hydroelectric and flood storage across multi-state watersheds.. Also the investment from the 1930s to the 1960s meant that the dams were built as an interconnected operational system where the upstream has a real impact on the downstream for flood timing. LL coldspots are mostly in the Desert and the great plains where topography and/or low run off and low populations make impoundment not economical.


b.
Notable HL tiles which are th high dam count surrounded by low count neighbors appear in Colorado and Central California. This is where intense irrigation infrastructure creates more localized dam infrastructure that doesn’t extend to surrounding areas (in the mountains). These highlight the agricultural lowlands that are abutted next to undeveloped mountainous regions. A large reservoir like Hoover Dam would also appear as a HL if it was next to super undeveloped wilderness. LH tiles which are sparse tiles surrounded by dense neighbors are more rare. These represent locations where dam construction may have been restricted by land status despite the hydrology or the area.


c.
The choice between hexagonal tiles and counties can produce different LISA maps. These differences are the most obvious at the edges as opposed to the actual hotspots and coldspots. The county tessellation generates more raw HH polygons just because los of countries provide more opportunity than the low hexagonal tiles. But they should be generally the same. The largest divergence is where the county boundaries cut through river corridors. A high density watershed that is split between two countries might show a much lower HL or LH as opposed the more accurate HH classification. the hexagonal accounts for this. This is the MAUP operating at the local scale. The administrative boundaries can introduce artifacts into the spatial process which is organized by hydrology rather than political boundaries. This is why the hexagonal LISA map is typically more reliable.


d.
The LISA output directly translates to the tiers management pririties across three categories. The HH hotspot regions (Tennessee, Ohio, New England) should be an area with much more multistate coordination efforts and emergency planning. If one dam were to collapse the downstream effects to each of these areas could be catastrophic. LL coldspot regions in the west represent much more simple management. Dams are isolated and far and few between and many are isolated. Comparing Q6 LISA results to Q4 purpose maps illustrate how the irrigation dam hotspots concentrate in the western US and the all-dam LISA hotspots are concentrated in the east. This means that there are fundamental differences in how the west needs to coordinate water management and how the east should.

# Reproject AOI to raster CRS
palo_utm <- st_transform(palo, crs(r))

# buffer to capture the river corridor around Palo (~5 km)

# Crop the stack
r_crop <- crop(r, vect(palo_buf))
r_crop

title: lab-04
text:
Question 2.

par(mfrow = c(2, 2))

# 1. Natural color: R-G-B (4-3-2)
plotRGB(r_crop, r = 4, g = 3, b = 2, stretch = "lin",
        main = "Natural color (R-G-B)")

# 2. Color infrared: NIR-R-G (5-4-3)
plotRGB(r_crop, r = 5, g = 4, b = 3, stretch = "lin",
        main = "Color infrared (NIR-R-G)")

# 3. False color water focus: NIR-SWIR1-R (5-6-4)
plotRGB(r_crop, r = 5, g = 6, b = 4, stretch = "lin",
        main = "False color water (NIR-SWIR1-R)")

# 4. Your choice — agriculture: SWIR1-NIR-B (6-5-2)
plotRGB(r_crop, r = 6, g = 5, b = 2, stretch = "lin",
        main = "Agriculture (SWIR1-NIR-B)")







par(mfrow = c(1, 1))


par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plotRGB(r_crop, r = 5, g = 4, b = 3, stretch = "lin",
        main = "Linear stretch")
plotRGB(r_crop, r = 5, g = 4, b = 3, stretch = "hist",
        main = "Histogram stretch")







par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

The RGB image is the closest to our actual vision. The rivers are visible as brown sediment laden water. The contrast between water and the soils is hard to detect. And flood delineation is really hard to see.
NIR-R-G image shows healthy vegetation with red colors because the plants reflect the NIR so strongly while absorbing water. The river channel stands out here against the red.
NIR-SWIR1-R image is really good at isolating the water since the water appears as dark blue since SWIR and NIR absorbs water. Vegetation stands out and so do the city houses. The contrast makes it a lot easier to see the flood extent.
SWIR1-NIR-B image is the agriculture one. I chose this one because of all the surrounding agriculture to Palo and the impact the ag is part of the story one might want to tell about the impacts of the flood. The SWIR band is sensitive to saturated soil so the submerged crops appear slightly darker.
The stretch comparison shows the linear stretch that has re-scaled the pixel values to the display range which not only preserves the brightness relationship but also creates a more natural looking image. The histogram stretch redistributed the pixel values to produce a more uniform intensity and makes more subtle features stand out more. But also can accentuate the noise.

     col = colorRampPalette(c("blue", "white", "red"))(256))








“Describe the 5 images. How are they similar and where do they deviate?”
All five of the images show the same flood feature which is just the main river channel. However, for NIR and SWIR vegetation is strongly reflected which is the sharp contrast. But NDVI is a vegetation index so it isn’t meant to actual detect water specifically.

“Describe the differences and similarities between the different maps.”
All five maps identify the same core flooding features and the channel which is N/S. The obvious overbank flooding is around the town of Palo. They all show where the actual channel is. How they differ is in the completeness. Some produced a very thin main channel, others captures the full extent of the overbank flooding.

“What do the dimensions of the extracted values tell you about how the data was extracted?”
The values function returned a matrix with 196,880 rows and 6 columns which corresponds to the 6 bands. Each column has the bands reflectance values across the image. The number of rows equals the number of pixels in the cropped raster. This means that values flattened the 3D spatial structure into a 2D matrix. The spatial arrangement is now in k-means with one observation per row per column.

run_kmeans <- function(v, r_template, k, seed = 123) {
  set.seed(seed)
  km <- kmeans(v, centers = k, iter.max = 100, nstart = 5)
  out <- r_template$coastal
  values(out) <- km$cluster
  out
}

agreement_nz <- agreement
agreement_nz[agreement_nz == 0] <- NA

The reason the cell values arent even numbers is the cell values in mapview aren’t anyways whole numbers even though agreement raster contains only integers from 0-6. The mapview doesn’t display the 30 m ratser grid full resolution and instead re-projects and resamples it. When zoomed out the 30 m pixels get averaged and those aren’t always whole numbers.
